content.text: An affordance is a design property that communicates to a user what an object can do — a button affords clicking, a door handle affords pulling. In AI interfaces, affordances answer the question users silently ask the moment they land on your product: "What can I do here, and what is this thing capable of?" Getting affordances wrong means users either underuse the AI (because they don't know it can help) or overtrust it (because they were led to believe it can do more than it can).

attrs.alt: Four types of AI affordances: input (placeholder text, example chips), capability (feature badges, skill chips), boundary (scope indicators, knowledge cutoff), and interaction (regenerate, edit, feedback). Anti-patterns vs good patterns comparison.

content.text: Input Affordances: Teaching Users How to Ask
The most common failure in AI product design is a blank text box with no guidance. The empty state should be as informative as the populated one. Three techniques work consistently:
Scoped placeholder text
. Instead of "Ask me anything", use "Ask me about your account, orders, or returns". Scoped placeholders set accurate expectations and reduce abandonment.
Starter chips

. Three to five clickable sample questions shown beneath the input box. Users click a chip to see a real AI response, building confidence before they type their own question. Pick chips that showcase the AI's most valuable capabilities.
Contextual follow-up suggestions

. After the AI responds, show 2–3 suggested follow-up queries relevant to what was just discussed. This patterns exists in most consumer AI products because it effectively prevents conversation dead-ends.
Capability Affordances: Showing What the AI Knows

If the AI has access to the user's data — their account, their files, their calendar — make this explicit. Users who don't know the AI has context will repeat information it already has, eroding trust in the product's intelligence.
Boundary Affordances: Setting Accurate Expectations

The AI cannot do everything users will try to ask. Surface these boundaries proactively rather than letting the AI apologize reactively. A "knowledge cutoff" label, a scope disclaimer, or a confidence indicator primes users to treat AI outputs appropriately.
Interaction Affordances: Teaching the Control Model

Many users do not know they can edit a previous message, regenerate a response, or tell the AI to try again with different constraints. These controls must be visible — not hidden in a tooltip — because users who don't see them conclude the AI is wrong and abandon.
Thinking and Loading Affordances

Users who submit a query and see nothing for 2–3 seconds will assume the interface is broken. A visible thinking state — animated dots, a loading message, a partial streaming response — maintains trust during generation latency.
Affordance Audit Checklist
Does the empty state tell users what to ask, not just "ask me anything"?
Are 3–5 starter chips visible before the user types their first message?
Does the header or info panel list the data the AI has access to?
Is there a thinking/loading state visible within 100ms of submission?
Are Regenerate, Copy, and feedback buttons visible on every AI response (not just on hover on desktop)?
When the AI cannot help, does it offer an alternative action (escalate to human, link to docs)?
Is there a knowledge cutoff or data-freshness label visible somewhere in the UI?
Can users stop a long-running generation?
